source.title: Sensorimotor Contingencies as a Key Drive of Development: From Babies to Robots

confidence_rationale: Well-specified review synthesizing infant and robot developmental data. Stage metrics (retention interval, reactivation window, generalization tolerance, means-end revaluation) are operationally concrete and cross-validated across species. Moderate transfer risk because the robot blueprint is architecturally schematic rather than implemented end-to-end. Mapping fidelity to INV-060 (play dominates child phase, progression by subsystem competence) is indirect -- the paper does not explicitly discuss play as the mechanism, only sensorimotor contingency sensitivity. Strong alignment with MECH-197 stage ordering via the 3-month, 6-month, 9-month, 24-month developmental pivots.
failure_signatures: The paper does not define discrete gated stages with formal threshold criteria -- progression is described as continuous accumulation of matching events, not as a pass/fail stage gate.

Competence-calibration separation is implicit, not formally defined: the finding that 7-month-olds have bidirectional contingency knowledge but cannot use it in action selection shows dissociation, but no metric distinguishes the two channels.
Blueprint architecture is schematic (Mannella 2018 instantiation) rather than a validated metric framework usable as a developmental readiness gate.

mapping.source_claim_statement: Sensitivity to sensorimotor contingencies drives four developmental domains -- body knowledge, memory, generalization, goal-directedness -- each with operationally measurable metrics: retention interval, reactivation window, generalization tolerance, revaluation success. Developmental pivots occur at 3, 6, 9, and 24 months. Competence (bidirectional contingency knowledge) dissociates from calibration (calibrated action selection) around 7 months.
mapping.ree_translation: The four domains map onto REE subsystems: body knowledge -> E1/E2 motor-sensory model, memory -> hippocampal replay, generalization -> z_world transferability, goal-directedness -> E3 trajectory selection under SD-012. The developmental pivots provide empirical grounding for INV-060's claim that play type progression tracks subsystem competence in dependency order. Competence-calibration dissociation at 7 months directly supports MECH-195 (strategy transfers, magnitude re-anchors at episode close) and the DEV-NEED-011 gate requiring strategy competence independent of synthetic calibration.
mapping.mapping_caveat: The paper addresses infant human/robot development, not play-stage gating specifically. The link from sensorimotor contingency to play type progression (INV-060, MECH-197) requires the additional assumption that sensorimotor play is the primary context in which these contingencies are exercised -- the paper does not establish this. The blueprint architecture is not demonstrated in a multi-stage system.
mapping.source_context: Infant behavioral paradigms (mobile contingency, mobile paradigm, means-end revaluation) + robotic blueprint review. Human infant age range 2-24 months.

confidence_components.notes: High source quality (Frontiers Neurorobotics, four author team including Baldassarre and Santucci who lead the ICDL/GRAIL research program). Moderate mapping fidelity because play is not the explicit frame -- sensorimotor contingency is the driver, and we infer play-stage mapping. Transfer risk is moderate: infant-to-robot transfer is the explicit goal of the paper, but REE's specific architecture (E1/E2/E3 with play frame tags) is more complex than the blueprint.
